speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape six of the interview with George Ginsburg on the 4th of September, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. May I introduce-- that's my daughter, Aviva-- Aviva Sophie. Sophie's after my mother. Aviva Sophie, she was born in 1973 in June, I think, and about three years-- three months-- before the Yom Kippur War in Israel. In Tel Aviv, she's born. And at the moment, she lives in Queensland and works in the hospitality business.

speaker: interviewee
text: Um, yes. I'm very proud of my father. He's been through a lot in his life. And one thing that I can say is that during the time, he's achieved a lot through his life. He's got out of the war in-- eventually. And he built himself up and had-- got married and had all of us, so I could thank him for that. Wasn't for my father, I wouldn't be here to now. But at least I've got something that my father's been through that's very sacred to me, and it means a lot to me, which I can pass on through generation to generation. And all my children and their children will always remember my father as the soldier that he was.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you tell us who the people are in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my grandfather-- my mother's father, Abram Polishchuk. That's my mother, in the middle. And that's my father on the right, Mark, and me. I was about six years of age. It must have been in 1929, '30. It's in [GERMAN], out of Berlin-- on a holiday resort. That's me, at six, seven years of age.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you tell us who the people are in this photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Certainly. That with a-- with a [NON-ENGLISH] there-- it was a chocolate bonbon-- that's the first day in school, in primary school. That's me on the right-- first day in school, on the right. On the left is Walter. That's my best friend. We grew up together. He, he never denied me because I was a Jew. And unfortunately, he finished up in a submarine on the bottom of the sea, during the war. He never returned.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know who the person is in the middle?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, just a school-- a school friend.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That must have been in 1929, '30.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Berlin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all rigt.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That photo shows you Regensburger Strasse in Berlin, where we lived, and Viktoria-Luise-Platz. on the right, which was a little place where I played. On the left, there's a block of flats where we lived. We had a whole apartment in there. And that's where I grew up. You can see the board in Berlin-- Regensburger Strasse, Charlottenburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get hold of this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was in Berlin about 12 months ago on a visit of the-- of the-- of the town hall, as a Berliner, on a-- on-- on a visit program. And I was invited by the mayor. And we were staying in hotels, and we went to all the places-- souvenir places and everything-- in our hometown where we grew up before we were forced to leave Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. Who's in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's myself. That was in 1938, just prior to the Crystal Night-- just short-- and just prior before me leaving Berlin. That's on my street in Regensburger Strasse, on my bicycle.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Just before I left Germany.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Would you describe this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] That was when I was accepted as a attached unit on the United States Forces, 3rd Army, Blackhawk division.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was it taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in 1945, after, after my escape from Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took the photo? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: The photo's been taken, one of the army guys. He took the photo for me. [LAUGHS] Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And you can see on the-- well, you can't see it. On the left sleeve-- if you'll put it a bit higher-- it's called Intelligence, on my shoulder.

speaker: interviewer
text: The-- the letter?

speaker: interviewee
text: There. There, there, there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: There. You can just read I-N-T.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: That one is Captain Klein, who was a doctor from my division-- as a matter of fact, one of the people who interrogated me as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is he the one that's standing up or sitting down?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's the one who's sitting down. The standing up is myself. And we, we go on a jeep from, from point A to point B. And somebody took the photo from us. And on the right-hand side is myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In [GERMAN] in Bavaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you know what year it was taken in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. In 1945-- end of 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Thank you. Unclear.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell us who's in this photo. That's Greg. That's my younger son, Greg. He's where-- he's a bodybuilder, and he's, he's, he's in the building business as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That has been taken in Berlin 12 months ago on a-- on a canoe going through the Spree, which is a river of Berlin. So he went with me to Berlin, visiting to see my, my past. And that's Greg, Greg Simon Ginsburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who's-- who is this in this photo?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my daughter Aviva. She's born in Tel Aviv. She's nearly 23, and she lives at the moment in Queensland. And she's in the hospitality business. So she's working in Capricorn Hotel over there, and she's visiting me today. I'm happy to be with her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was taken a couple of years ago, I think, if I'm not mistaken.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know where it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHING] No, not exactly.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: On a graduation or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good, OK. Thank you. Who's the person--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my--

speaker: interviewer
text: No, go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my daughter, Tali. She's my baby. She's, at the moment, in Jerusalem. She finished [NON-ENGLISH]. She immigrated to Israel a year ago. She was on Machon before, and she liked it very much. So she came back here, stayed here with me, and went to Israel for good. And she's studying at a university in Jerusalem. She's studying biology and immunotherapy.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: That were taken before she left. That must be about a year ago, a year and a half ago.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Melbourne?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Melbourne. And now she is in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thank you. Now, he wants one photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: Tell us about this photo. Well, that's my oldest son, Mark. And he's about 25-- or more, 28. And that's his son, Danny.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old's--

speaker: interviewee
text: Danny Boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old's Danny?

speaker: interviewee
text: Danny Boy's three years old now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's my grandson.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old is the photograph? Do you know when it was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, about a couple of years ago. He's in Adelaide now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was this one taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think that was taken in my backyard in Doncaster somewhere, before I had this house here.

speaker: interviewer
text: So that was in Melbourne.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about this document.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, that document has been issued by the RSL of Australia. And on the right-hand side is a Good Conduct Medal, which was issued to me by the United States Forces. On the left is a photo of mine. It was identification. And that's a certificate who hangs on my wall. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And when was this certificate given to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was given to me in-- I think it's in 1950 or something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what about the medal? When was that given to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: The medal was given to me in '47 before-- end of '46 in Germany-- was issued to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Thank you.
